content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Any film about a weird girl?

**Score:** 187
**Body:** I am sick and tired of watching films where the "weird girl" is this pretty chick who is just awkward, socially inept, lonely, and nerdy, who magically always seems to have guys attracted to her. I want proper weird girls who are actually weird that the people around her are scared of her or weirded out by her. I want a film that actually has a weird girl and surrounds her life. Any suggestions of any genre is welcomed, thanks

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Muriel's Wedding (1994) Toni Collette's break out role as possible the weirdest girl you'll see on screen lmao. It's great.
2. Welcome to the Dollhouse
  - Instantly what I thought of too.

Edit: It's not a movie, but "Dead Like Me" might also fit the bill.
3. May (2002) is exactly what you're looking for - Angela Bettis plays this genuinely disturbing loner who collects body parts to make a "friend" and everyone is legitimately creeped out by her, not charmed
4. Dinner in America
5. Little Miss Sunshine.  The entire family is heart breaking/hilarious at once.
6. Carrie
7. 'Welcome to the Dollhouse' (1995)

'Ghost World' (2001)
8. Tank Girl, Harold and Maude
9. Ingrid goes west

## 2. Freaky Tales (2025) - If you're anti-nazi, pro-revolution, feelin' kinda like the idea of the catharsis of watching skinheads and nazis die horribly, you're sleeping on this movie.

**Score:** 85
**Body:** An omnibus of interconnected short stories set in one neighborhood in California in 1987. Unabashedly anti-nazi, anti-racist, and absolutely revels in making bad guys bad, good guys badder.  Watch it.

Beware that this movie revels in depicting scenes of Nazi deaths with the kind of giddy revelry that would make Tarantino wonder if maybe it's going a bit too far, but it is pure catharsis.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Interesting that the biggest star had the weakest scenes, but it was good fun and it’s nice to see this sort of old school midnight movie. This sort of release feels increasingly rare.
  - I didn't even see him with a producer credit or anything, which suggests that he just wanted to be a part of the project. Thought it was neat, believed in the message, I dunno.
    - Your talking about the 'movie guy' right?

He grew up in the Bay Area and went to high school in Oakland.  The movie is very much an homage to late 80s Oakland.
      - Oh shoot! How could I have called Pedro the biggest star!? You’re right, I’m a dummy. Also gotta love a movie with built in movie recs! Have you seen em all? I hadn’t seen The Verdict till after this nudge and I dug it! Breaking Away rules if you haven’t seen that one.
        - Yeah, I was a little confused by your comment when you said "scenes", because I was thinking, the biggest star in this movie only has 1 scene...

I thought Pedro's scenes were just fine, I actually didn't really enjoy the Too $hort concert segment.

When I was a teenager back then I LOVED Too $hort... Haven't really listened to his music in 30 years and watching this film REALLY made me realize how poorly his lyrics have aged...

The 80s were a different time though...  I digress
      - I low key hated how much he's got me pegged, though. "I'm trying to give you an *experience*," and being quietly offended when they don't take his suggestions 😄
        - I read awhile back that the filmmakers referenced him working at a video store in high school a few times in the early script writing.  They finally just asked if he would be in it.  And they gave his character the perfect name!
  - I noticed that too big names felt muted but the vibe carried it old school grindhouse energy and no sympathy for nazis made it a fun watch overall
2. Hell yeah, been looking for something with that kind of energy - sounds like it scratches that Inglourious Basterds itch but in a different setting
3. Its a fun watch, unique, unpredictable

## 3. Destroy me

**Score:** 78
**Body:** I'm depressed and desperate for a movie that will destroy me. No happy endings. I wanna end up sobbing like a baby

P.s. I've already seen Manchester by the Sea. Vibed with it and didn't want this movie to end, loved it. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’ve heard dear Zachary will do the trick. I havent brought myself to watch it yet. But I’ll also suggest paddleton. And one I personally like when I’m depressed is Virgin suicides.
2. Aftersun
3. It is my duty to make sure these threads always include Dancer in the Dark. I can only watch this movie if I have no plans the following day because my eyes swell nearly shut from sobbing so hard.
4. What Dreams May Come - Saddest fucking movie of my life with one of the funnest people to ever walk the earth.
5. "Come and see"
6. Grave of the Fireflies will absolutely wreck you, just have tissues ready because that one hits different
7. Where the red fern grows if you haven’t seen it yet. Such a classic.
8. Brokeback Mountain
9. Leaving Las Vegas

The Basketball Diaries

The Pianist

Dead Poet's Society
10. Marley and Me.

## 4. Looking for the absolute worst "Vanity Project" movies ever made.

**Score:** 42
**Body:** "Vanity Project" movies are typically backed by the actor who stars in the movie. Often these movies are written, directed, produced, and financed by the star of the movie. These movies are often hilariously terrible, which is why I have a strange habit of trying to see how many minutes of them I can get through before calling it quits. I then go look for highlight videos on YouTube because I couldn't finish the movie. Examples include:

The Room starring Tommy Wiseau, which I actually finished.

All movies by Neil Breen: Double Down, I am Here Now, Fateful Findings, Pass Thru, Twisted Pair, Cade

Champagne and Bullets by John De Hart

Rock n Roll Nightmare with Jon Mikl Thor

Miami Connection by YK Kim

Blackbird by Michael Flatley (Riverdance guy)

Cool as Ice by Vanilla Ice. I tried this and couldn't finish it.

Battlefield Earth with John Travolta.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Battlefield Earth. I know you already listed it, but it deserves to be listed twice; once for how terrible it is and once for the amount of vanity involved.
  - Winner. 

It's not so bad it's good. It's so bad it's REALLY REALLY BAD.  I used to pride myself on being a media masochist who could find the fun in everything. Battlefield Earth proved me wrong. Its like it's designed on purpose to be repellent.
2. The answer is "Trapped in the Closet" the full length musical by R. Kelly. Bonus points if you can find the DVD commentary.  It's so unintentionally hilarious that it's fantastic!
3. Most recently, Francis Ford Coppolas Megalopolis 
  - Most recently is Melania
4. Nothing but Trouble - I like Dan Ackroyd but this was a trainwreck of a movie
5. Glitter with Maria Carey!
6. How about that recent-ish J-Lo fantasy-autobio-concert-ish movie…

Uh…”This Is Me”…or “This One’s Mine”…??? …or something like that…

🤷🏾‍♂️🤷🏾‍♂️🤮
7. That Melania Trump movie that just premiered, the very definition of a vanity project.
8. On Deadly Ground (1994) A blowout firefighter takes on an environmentally destructive oil corporation by causing many deaths and explosions in Alaska.  Even if you can't make it through the whole thing, fast forward to the big speech at the end.  It's a... well, it's something.

## 5. “Anthropology” Movie Night Suggestions?

**Score:** 34
**Body:** i’m a student in anthropology and would like to host film nights that are fun movies but we can talk about it after. i’m looking for movies that talk about social issues or things that may be interesting to discuss with an anthropological lens - but they do not have to be “anthropology movies”.

For example, we met up once already and watched Wall-e and talked about consumerism, what it means to live, and the relationship between humans and the environment. It was also just a fun movie to watch!

Please give me any and all recommendations!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Gods Must Be Crazy - is technology a boon or a blight?
2. The Banshees of Inisherin: how people reason themselves into isolation and war


Blade Runner 2049: what it means to be human 


The Zone of Interest: the banality of evil


Saw another comment recommending it but Aguirre the wrath of god too
3. * Rise of the Planet of the Apes (2011).
* Ex Machina (2014).
* Arrival (2016).


All of these should give science-minded people something to talk about, especially with humans and their relationships to animals/machines/aliens. And they are all good movies!
4. Try out some of  Werner Herzog\`s films such as:                                                        *Aguire:Wrath of God,                                                                                                                      Grizzly Man,                                                                                                                        Fitzcaraldo,                                                                                                                              Where the Green Ants Dream*
5. Dune Part 1 and 2

Blade Runner and Blade Runner 2049

The Man from Earth

Nomadland

Fight Club

Akira

Django Unchained

Snowpiercer

Sinners
6. City of God (2002).  

Description:  https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/City_of_God_(2002_film)
7. Baraka and Samsara

Princess Mononoke 

The Constant Gardener
8. Embrace of the Serpent 

You'll thank me later!

  
Also: Samsara
9. A lot of themes to explore in Annihilation
10. Krippendorf’s Tribe    

The Gods Must Be Crazy

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Rest in peace

**Score:** 1991

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hope she’s enjoying a good drink with John Candy and singing polka tunes.
  - "Polka, Polkaaaa!, Polka!" "No? Never heard of it?"
2. This sucks, RIP Mrs. Mcallister
3. Sad when they go so young.
  - 71. A fuckin kid.
4. NOOO.  I just told 2025 to fuck off.  Its too early to tell 2026 that.
5. RIP MOIRA ROSE!!!
6. Devastating! 
  
One of the biggest talents Canada (and North America quite frankly) has ever produced
7. Home Alone ended officially. RIP a beautiful soul.
8. funny woman. and a beautiful voice - she and her sister. lovely ladies, both!

## 2. Most iconic Catherine O'Hara role

**Score:** 546
**Body:** Kevin's mom in Home Alone

Moira in Schitt's Creek \[TV\]

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Home Alone is probably her most iconic. 

Cookie Fleck in Best In Show is comic genius. 

But my favorite is her small role in After Hours as Gail.

RIP. 😢
2. Beetle juice
  - Respect
3. Moira Rose.
  - DAVID!!
4. Her best role is easily
Best in show
And it's not even a competition
  - Best in Show was in fact a competition.
5. Bam Bam Funkhouser
6. Lola heatherton - SCTV  (tv)
Cookie Fleck - Best in Show (film)
7. “KEVIN!!!” That facial expression and voice is so iconic. As a fellow parent, I have those moments. RIP.

## 3. I just saw Bugonia

**Score:** 480
**Body:** Guys, I just saw Bugonia for the first time, and I didn't know what to expect, but the ending of this movie left me perplexed and full of anxiety.

I don't want to spoil it for anyone who hasn't seen it yet, but for God's sake, if that's the case, please watch it today.

I don't think I've ever seen Jesse Plemons as brilliant as in this film, and he's a tremendous actor. Emma Stone, as always, impeccable. This type of role suits her perfectly.

Excellent movie.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. When life gives you Plemmons, you watch.
  - You also make Plemmonade
2. Jesse Plemons may be the best actor doing it right now, he is really in a golden moment. This was the best male performance of 2025, and it's not particularly close. 

Bugonia is spectacular.
  - Agreed. I was really surprised he didn't get an Oscar nomination for this role.
    - Kirsten was ready to throw hands.
    - It’s insulting
3. I loved this movie so much I'm going to be watching it again this weekend with my roommate. 

The actor that played Don - this was his first movie. Holy shit, he killed it!
4. Plemmons is very good at delivering dialogue in such a relaxed and natural way.  
5. Plemons didn't get an Oscar nomination for this..... that's criminal.
6. I really wanted that ending and was not disappointed.

## 4. Finally watched “Fantastic Planet”…ughh.

**Score:** 223
**Body:** For years I’ve been told it’s one of those cult films from a prior era whose impact can still be felt like a ripple across a pond. 

I am just absolutely baffled at the love this movie is given by critics and film historians.

The best adjective to describe how I felt was “underwhelmed”. While I can appreciate the dreamlike cadence and unique atmosphere; I was just pausing the screen over and over just to check the time remaining. It’s just so boring.

Depending on who writes/critiques it; the writer will say it’s an allegory for either racism or unethical treatment of animals. I agree with the unethical animal treatment debate. 

To conclude; I feel this movie’s whimpering energy and heavy-handed messaging were probably lauded by their peers who attended foreign film circuits in major eastern/western seaboard cities in the 70’s - but ultimately I feel retrospective criticism and analysis can serve to help topple older views in cinema that are held as unnecessarily sacrosanct. 

Also, I’m unapologetically racist to blue aliens and I want them all dead. Navi, Tau, Draags…all of them go into a woodchipper feet first. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don't want to argue that you were wrong to dislike the film, nor would I argue against it being weak in a traditional sense - the story is fairly basic, the characterization nonexistent, the moralizing heavy-handed, and the pacing almost aggressively slow.

However, I think in some ways that is intentional. The film is supposed to be dislocating, sedative, almost dissociative. Obviously it's popular with the drug afficionados because it seems to slow down time and suspend you in a profoundly alien universe. The tedium you experienced becomes a kind of endlessness, which in turn becomes enveloping. The temporal pacing of a regular film - which subtly cues you to where you are in the film's overall progress - is abandoned so you feel as though it might never end - that you are abandoned to this new and incomprehensible universe.

And I'd argue it does something well that is incredibly challenging in Science Fiction - genuine alienness. Most science fiction aliens are basically just human archetypes in rubber suits - and for the most part, this is what we prefer. Films like Arrival try to depict the profound gulf between us and beings that evolved in an utterly different world and whose otherness is essentially apodictic.

I'm not arguing that the Draags are imagined with such inventiveness - they are just archetypes in big rubber suits - but there is some seriously unsettling freakiness to their society, their technology, and their flora and fauna. It's monstrous in a Bosch fashion. And that dislocation produced is compounded by the soupy, sedative electronic music, the evaporation of emotion, and the absence of normal time markers.

And again, to be absolutely clear - it's totally legitimate to reject all this because it goes against your aesthetic criteria. I, for example, insist that visionary art fundamentally superior to prestige art, and therefore Apocalypse Now is categorically better than The Godfather. That's my aesthetic system.

I also dislike Bergman - but I respect him. I can see what he's trying to do, but I don't agree with his ideas about what art should be. But I also don't think other people are wrong to like him.

In short, I think one can appreciate this film - an in particular its uniquely trippy atmosphere and dislocatingly-weird aesthetic, and still dislike it. But I do think it absolutely has value to a certain audience.

Edit: I meant to say "asymptotic" and not "apodictic" and even though the latter term still works, I hope readers will appreciate the cautionary tale about the dangers of operating heavy vocabulary while drinking.
  - Man. If only all folks on Reddit were as considered and thought out in replies. Great comment!
2. Drugs help
3. Watched this the other day. Kinda cool
4. I love this movie. I was in high school and discovered it late at night flipping through channels and suddenly this very strange thing appears, nothing like any movie I’d ever seen.
5. I loved how different it is.
6. If you expected a well paced plot, you went into it wrong. It's experimental French animation with a non-obvious slow moving plot from the 70s.

I understand not wanting to finish it, but no appreciation for the animation and originality?
7. I have a Draag tattooed on me. Love the OST :)
8. Sorry you didn't enjoy it man. I love it dearly. I'm 40 years old and I first watched this as a kid. My Stepdad owned 1000s of VHS tapes and this was one of them that I watched multiple times. I was completely fascinated with it then, and still am now as I watch the Criterion Bluray at least once a year. We can't all love the same stuff equally or else humanity would be a boring, soulless hivemind.
9. Nah it’s good. And the score is even better. 

## 5. Can something be legitimately called a Documentary if the subject has complete creative control and was paid!

**Score:** 140
**Body:** Seems like calling the new Melania Puff Piece a Documentary is completely ridiculous!

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You won't be laughing when she gets a FIFA Oscar.
2. The true name of this is “propaganda”
  - “Money laundering”
  - Reality TV movie and bribe, mixed with propaganda.
3. It’s called propaganda. See: the works of Joseph Goebbels for more examples.
4. Did you expect anything truthful from that fucking family.
5. I feel like half the big documentaries recently on Netflix are like this. Vanity documentaries aren’t new but they are all lame.
6. Documentaries are not required to be truthful.  They are presented that way, people tend to expect them to be that way, but they are not required to be...

[https://www.ebsco.com/research-starters/history/documentary-film](https://www.ebsco.com/research-starters/history/documentary-film)

"The term documentary was coined by Scottish filmmaker John Grierson (1898–1972) in an article for the *New York Sun* in 1926. Grierson defined the documentary as "the creative treatment of actuality," and his definition is still generally accepted. "

So in practical use the label 'documentary' just ends up meaning 'a story about real events'.  The events themselves are non fiction, like she really is the first lady, and they likely included clips from the real events, but a great deal of editorial / creative storytelling was applied to the process.
7. If this were a real documentary, it would be interesting.  The story of an Eastern European slut prostituting her way to America, and sleeping with some rich old pervs (including Epstein) until she settled on the orange idiot. Probably highly entertaining story.

What it's going to be is some puff-piece trying to portray the old slut as "classy," which is sickening.
  - I'm holding my breath that in 30 years an *actual* biopic of the Epstein, Trump, Melania, Miss America scandal comes to fruition...

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Sinners (2025) and modern "prestige" or "elevated" movies being a symptom of streaming services and other changes to the film landscape

**Score:** 64
**Body:** # [https://open.substack.com/pub/avadwyer6/p/sinners-what-ruined-modern-prestige?utm\_campaign=post&utm\_medium=email](https://open.substack.com/pub/avadwyer6/p/sinners-what-ruined-modern-prestige?utm_campaign=post&utm_medium=email)

  
I wrote an essay on the ways streaming services, institutional/economic/cultural shifts have changed film in the last fifteen years. I used Sinners (which I liked as a whole) as an example of this new type of movie that affects prestige aesthetics but is conceptually engagement-oriented and performing/gesturing at depth without committing. Would love to hear any thoughts or discussion. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. eh, i wouldnt call Sinners a "prestige" movie because the movie is way messier and dives into a lot of genre stabs to be in that category. For me it feels like calling a Zahler movie like Dragged Across Concrete  a "prestige movie", it feels like a very lazy criticism based around the fact that those would try to communicate some grand statement (that it isnt any really grand).

I wouldnt also call Coogler repetition a lack of faith in audience because repetition of themes it isnt any new in filmmaking (and specially american filmmaking). Guys like Carpenter and De Palma made a lot of those movie that had a hammer approach towards audiences and what they wanted to make. Specially De Palma who lifted a lot of Hawks and Hitchcock across his career.

Also idk why you put that you liked the film. It is in the text very obvious that you think Sinners is kinda phony. Just say it.
  - De Palma reveled in a kind of trashiness that made him anathema to the academy and other general markers of “prestige,” I wouldn’t put his films into the same camp as something like *Sinners* at all even if there are superficial similarities. 

If anything Sinners speaks to the process by which filmmakers, like De Palma, who existed on the periphery of prestige in their day, are metabolized (and to some extent neutered) into “good taste,” and processed into “influences,” on works which then actually go on to reside in “prestige” spaces. 

These types of discussions are less about films per se and more about the relationship between films, audiences, and the industry, and the way filmmakers communicate certain expectations and “trappings,” (whether they be genre trappings or prestige trappings), through their film’s form and positioning within the context of the medium.
2. I watched Sinners last night. I liked the movie a lot, but I concur with your assessment of the heavy-handed thematic development and excessive audience hand-holding. Similar to the flashback to Smoke and Annie’s child’s grave, there are a few clear foreshadowing scenes early in the film that they unnecessarily flash back to when they come to fruition as if a Usual Suspects-level twist is revealed. For instance, when the racist good ol’ boy sells the barn to the twins and tells them the Klan isn’t around anymore, it’s obvious he’s lying and that his comment is ominous. So when he and the other Klansmen show up in the morning, why flashback to him making the comment to the twins? Only the most obtuse of viewers wouldn’t make the connection.
3. Yeah I agree with a lot of this on the whole. I think the affectation you mention is a good summary of how I felt about the movie. It postures like a great prestige work but really it’s not as conceptually or emotionally deep as I would like. It’s very comfortable in its simplicity. Unambiguous in its vision and morality. For it to be nominated for the most Oscars ever is a sign of how things have changed. It’s a very well made film, well directed and well performed but I don’t think it will be remembered as some breakthrough masterpiece that the 16 nominations would suggest it is. Ultimately it’s just that bit too derivative.
  - It getting the most Oscar’s ever is more of a structural phenomenon. 

It is a highly spectacle driven genre film but has also positioned itself as a prestige drama. Straddling those two lines allowed it to get nominations in all of the technical awards as well as the acting/directing/writing categories (add in the dearth of original big films in Hollywood, you can see how Sinners/Coogler are well positioned to be recognized for their originality). Typically films get a lot of nominations in one of the two “subsections” so to speak. 

Then you add in the fact that it has an original song, attached to it, it gives you all the necessary components for it to eke out those extra nominations out over past big films that straddled both technical and traditional categories like Titanic.
  - I don't see how it postures as a prestige work at all. I think it's pretty clearly populist in its approach and unashamed about it. It's greatness comes from precisely that. It's not trying to be some complex boundary pushing work. It just uses a lot of genre traditions in a unique blend to create a fun movie that has some depth and moral value behind it still. Film is not just an art form but also entertainment, so it's ok for movies to take more of an approach that's just about entertainment rather than a challenging art type of thing.
    - I agree. Coogler’s approach is really refreshing and I’d rather see more movies that take creative risks and have unique concepts (along with actual themes and meaning) than the pile of biopics, period dramas, family dramas, dramedies that are extremely light on the comedy, etc that that we’re guaranteed to get every year. 

Also, comparing Sinners to Silence of the Lambs and Rosemarys baby like she does in the essay isn’t really fair cause they’re going for totally different tones and types of horror. Sinners would have been kindof ridiculous if it treated its vampire-musical premise with that level of groundedness.
4. Your assertions about "thinly veiled metaphor" in reference to Blues appropriation seems counter to your previous point of telling and not showing. Those thinly veiled metaphors are exactly the kind of audience information to be trusted with, that you crticize in the previous paragraph. The use of those thin metaphors are used more effectively as extended metaphor than the credit given here. 


Besides that it is a thoughtful article and I agree with a lot of the criticism, but I also don't elevate Sinners to some high art beyond the direction. I do think it's simultaneously a fun and thoughtful blockbuster (which we used to have aplenty). Those did exist before the "either or, or both" options you argue we have now. Somehow we have far less of those than we do art house/indie successes. 


I wish Sinners of all movies didn't become the whipping boy for the state of film and the "line" for artistic merit, but it got 16 Oscar noms so everybody can have at it.

Edit: I reread my comment and it's frankly not thoughtful enough for the time and effort you put into your essay. I'll leave it cause, but I don't like it.
5. It is honesty baffling to me when people talk about Sinners as a thematically simplistic movie. Here is an excerpt of a review by Isabella Von Ghoul exploring the movie’s themes. I highly doubt most viewers got all the following from watching it.

“ But what truly captivates me is its core message, which explores two intertwined themes: Black liberation and white alienation.

The Black characters in Sinners are acutely aware of their place in the Jim Crow South—the unspoken rules dictating where they work, who they love, and how they navigate a world built to suppress them. Music becomes their refuge, a lineage stretching from African traditions to blues, bluegrass, and beyond. It’s a means of transforming pain into power, of forging connection in the face of oppression. Yet, as history shows, white America has repeatedly co-opted Black music, stripping it of its origins while deriding its creators. The film asks: Why this relentless theft? White musicians have their own folk traditions—Irish ballads, European harmonies—so why the insatiable hunger to colonize Black sound?

[SPOILERS AHEAD]

The answer lies in Remmick, the Irish vampire antagonist. He craves Sammie’s musical gift, which holds the power to bridge past, present, and future. Remmick is lonely, severed from his ancestors, his people—his very identity. Vampirism, here, is a metaphor for cultural disconnection. He converts others into his hive mind, but no amount of followers can fill the void left by his lost heritage.

In contrast, Wunmi Mosaku’s Annie, a Hoodoo practitioner, remains rooted in her spiritual traditions—a fusion of African, Indigenous, and Christian beliefs. Her connection to the past is her strength, a shield against the vampires’ predation. White supremacy, like vampirism, demands the same sacrifice: to assimilate is to sever ties with one’s ancestry, language, and culture. In that surrender, white people lose too. There’s no room for Irish dance or song in a system built on erasure—only hollow conformity, a living death. When Christianity supplanted Celtic traditions in Ireland, it was its own kind of vampirism, a cultural severance that bred generations of alienation in Irish Americans”
6. I just watched it yesterday, and was underwhelmed to be honest. I think you captured more eloquently than I described it as to my wife as basically a vampire movie, where the racism etc is so heavy handedly spelled out it doesn't really matter. It wasn't a bad movie, just I don't understand how it is an Oscar contender. Mind you we didn't much like One Battle After Another either, so... no idea who we're rooting for for Best Picture...

Frankly I feel like Shondaland's The Residence had more to say about racism in America than Sinners did...

## 2. What are your thoughts on Die My Love? And Lynne Ramsay's Movies?

**Score:** 20
**Body:** Just writing things that stuck with me  
  
Grace: I'm Stuck between wanting to do something and wanting to do nothing at all

Jackson: I'm getting really stressed  
Grace: About what?

What amazing dialogues really captures the moment and are a solid punch in the gut, The two films I have watched of Lynne Ramsay "We need to talk about Kevin" and this I have found a similar meaning or if I must say a message, a message to let go, a message that not everything needs to be resolved or fixed, sometimes the things or people are just the way they are, and also she has convinced me not to have a child.

Thanks Lynne Ramsay :)

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think it’s by far her worst film, and I would attribute that to the fact that the project didn’t originate with her. During the press tour, we were told again and again that it was Scorsese’s idea to bring the book to Jennifer Lawrence, who then went looking for a director. There are some beautiful shots, but I found the whole endeavor to be empty and forgettable.
2. I felt let down. I think I already wrote on this in another thread, but it just felt like a major lack of nuance and not much character arc. 

JLaw and RPats crush their performances, which is no surprise, but it's just kind of the same thing over and over again. JLaw acts weird, does some stupid shit, rinse and repeat. We're like, supposed to feel bad for her, I guess, because post-partum and whatever, but the film also sort of implies that RPats is cheating on her, but never really takes a stance on it. 

And then she runs off into the flames at the end, and it's like...huh? But why? It sort of starts to try and become a surrealist movie in the last 10 minutes or something. 

I really wanted it to work. I like everyone involved, and I liked the look too, but in the end it's just a depressing piece of work that really has nothing to say. Even if she's depressed, she's not sympathetic. She behaves terribly. The film takes no stance on anything. What am I supposed to get out of it? The fact that some women get depressed after they give birth? Okay. I know that. But the film really almost takes the stance that having children is terrible and women shouldn't do it. 

I dunno. The script needed work if you ask me.

Stalking through the reeds. A knife warm in your palm. The drone of flies. Dreams of a rockstar. The fridge cold against your spine. Wallpaper underneath your fingernails. Grass brushing your ankles. A pushchair trailing dust. Pages still blank. Cake too sweet. Smoke against your skin.

## 3. People who have seen the original Save The Green Planet (2003), did you like Bugonia?

**Score:** 14
**Body:** I love the original Korean movie so was very excited to watch Bugonia especially with its great reviews.

\*Original film aside\* I didn’t find the film enjoyable.

The original had so much silliness, goofiness in not just concept but prop, set design, acting, and at the same time there was so much suspense and discomfort. It had me on the edge of my seat while giggling like a kid and saying that’s so silly!

I think it’s an fun attempt when the remake doesn’t exactly copy an original, I don’t think that is the problem. but Bugonia felt neither silly (although it had the same silly themes like kidnapping a potential alien and shaving its head to not let it contact its planet) nor did it make me contemplate humanity (which I felt like was more of a take they were intending on - discussion of other species like dinosaurs and bees and how their complex society is admired, the unraveling of how the current humanity came to be in the end, depiction of classism and capitalism, the policeman who formally abused the boy, conspiracy theorists / mental illness). It also didn’t make me feel uncomfortable, or scared. It just felt like a shade of colour that didn’t hit. Am I missing something here? I’m not a hater but I’m a little confused to how the rating is so good. The film didn’t hold on my attention

## 4. What if the Cannes Film Festival hadn't been cancelled in 1968?

**Score:** 4
**Body:** For context, the Cannes Film Festival was supposed to happen in 1968 from 10-24 May, and it did initially start out as normal. However, this edition had been marred with controversy months in advance due to the removal of Henri Langlois from the Cinémathèque Française at the hands of the government's Ministry of Culture and National Cinema Centre and a large vote against from French New Wave figures like François Truffaut.

What ended up doing the festival in, though, was Mai 68, a massive wave of protests all across France that, combined with the weariness that French New Wave directors were feeling, led to the festival getting cancelled mid-way through (officially on the 19th, though the last film shown was on the 17th). However, what if the boards decided to go through with it, anyways, or there simply weren't protests in the first place & Langlois kept his position?

I originally titled this to ask who would've won the Palme d'Or, but I'd like to invite more broad discussion. In addition to asking who you think likely would've won the Palme d'Or, what do you think the best film from the official (competitive) selection was? Lastly, if you so desire, apply these to the other awards given away (such as the Grand Prix and Prix du Jury).

## 5. 'Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid' (1969) Revolutionized Modern Filmmaking and William Goldman is an All-Time Screenwriter

**Score:** 5
**Body:** This film is masterfully written and I believe it is the quintessential work of legendary screenwriter William Goldman, capturing the heart of his clever writing style.  Same writer of  'All the President's Men' (1976) and 'Marathon Man' (1976), who also wrote the novel and follow-on screenplay for 'The Princess Bride' (1987).  Two-time Best Screenplay Oscar winner.  Literant without being too literary and pretentious.  


'Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid' is singularly enchanting and charming, distinct from any movie I've seen.  Maybe the most impactful "modern" western that was pivotal in establishing the groundwork for future  contemporary films of this genre and film overall.  Revolutionary.  The soundtrack is ethereal and the writing is beautiful.  Paul Newman and Robert Redford have to be among the two most talented actors with the most chemistry to ever appear together on the big screen (see 'The Sting').  It's a movie filled with one iconic scene after another, set behind truly captivating landscapes.  


"What're you crazy?  The fall will probably kill ya!" 


Burt Bacharac's score in this is amazing.
